description:
You are the freshly-appointed site administrator for an internal Linux server.  
  A co-worker left behind three small flat-files that describe existing and
  pending user accounts.  Unfortunately the files are messy: some users are
  inactive, some records are malformed, and there is no single consolidated
  list of the active accounts.

  Directory layout that already exists when you start:
    /home/user/data/users/alpha.txt
    /home/user/data/users/beta.txt
    /home/user/data/users/gamma.txt

  The exact original contents are shown below (do **not** edit these files):

    ── /home/user/data/users/alpha.txt ──────────────────────────────────────────
    alice,alice@example.com,1001,true
    bob,bob@example,1002,TRUE
    carol,carol@example.com,1003,false
    ── /home/user/data/users/beta.txt ──────────────────────────────────────────
    dave,dave@example.com,1004,true
    eve,eve@example.com,,true
    frank,frank@example.com,1006,TRUE
    ── /home/user/data/users/gamma.txt ─────────────────────────────────────────
    grace,grace@example.com,1007,false
    heidi,heidi@sample.com,1008,true
    invalidlinewithoutcommas
    ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  Your goal is to build a **recoverable data-pipeline** completely from the
  command line that:

    1. Reads every line from every “*.txt” file in /home/user/data/users.
    2. Treats a record as *valid* when all four comma-separated fields are
       present **and each field is non-empty**.
       • field-0 → username  
       • field-1 → e-mail  
       • field-2 → uid  
       • field-3 → active flag (case-insensitive string “true”/“false”)
    3. Keeps only the records whose active flag is “true” (case-insensitive).
    4. Sorts the remaining active users alphabetically by the username.
    5. Produces three new artefacts inside /home/user/output (create the
       directory if necessary):
       5a) active_users.csv   – exact CSV described below  
       5b) pipeline_errors.log – detailed error/skip log described below  
       5c) summary.json       – one-line JSON summary described below

  Output file formats that **the automated grader will check byte-for-byte**:

    1. /home/user/output/active_users.csv
       • MUST start with the header line:
           username,email,uid
       • MUST list exactly one line per valid active account, with the three
         fields in that order, *no* surrounding spaces.
       • MUST be sorted in ascending ASCII order of the username.
       • MUST NOT contain duplicate usernames.
       • MUST end with a trailing newline (Unix LF).

       For the input supplied above the final correct file **must contain
       exactly these 6 lines**:

         username,email,uid
         alice,alice@example.com,1001
         bob,bob@example,1002
         dave,dave@example.com,1004
         frank,frank@example.com,1006
         heidi,heidi@sample.com,1008

    2. /home/user/output/pipeline_errors.log
       • Every line that is *not* written to active_users.csv *must* appear in
         this log.
       • Prefix inactive records with “INACTIVE: ”  
         Prefix malformed/invalid records with “ERROR: ”.
       • Preserve the *original* raw record after the prefix.
       • Keep the records in the same order they were encountered while
         reading the source files.
       • End the file with a trailing newline (Unix LF).

       With the given data the log **must contain exactly these 4 lines**:

         INACTIVE: carol,carol@example.com,1003,false
         INACTIVE: grace,grace@example.com,1007,false
         ERROR: eve,eve@example.com,,true
         ERROR: invalidlinewithoutcommas

    3. /home/user/output/summary.json
       • A single JSON object on one line, followed by a newline.
       • Keys and integer values:
           total_lines  – total number of raw records read
           valid_active – number of lines written to active_users.csv
           inactive     – number of inactive (valid but not active) lines
           invalid      – number of malformed/invalid lines
       • Whitespace: exactly one space after every colon, none elsewhere.

       For the supplied inputs the summary must be **exactly**:

         {"total_lines": 9, "valid_active": 5, "inactive": 2, "invalid": 2}

  Robustness & re-run requirements:

    • The pipeline must be idempotent: running it multiple times must yield
      the same three output files *without* duplicate data or additional log
      entries.
    • The script/command chain you write should fail fast if any step errors
      (e.g., bad glob, permissions, etc.) and leave partial files **only** if
      the previous run succeeded.
    • Make sure the /home/user/output directory exists before writing, or
      create it as part of your solution.

  Deliverables the autograder verifies:

    • /home/user/output/active_users.csv       (exactly as specified)
    • /home/user/output/pipeline_errors.log    (exactly as specified)
    • /home/user/output/summary.json           (exactly as specified)

  You may compose the pipeline however you like (awk, grep, sed, sort, bash
  script, etc.), but **do not remove or alter** the original files under
  /home/user/data/users.  Feel free to create additional helper scripts inside
  your home directory if that simplifies your work.

  When you are done, the grader will inspect the three deliverables for exact
  content and verify the absence of duplicates in active_users.csv.

  (Reminder: The replay environment does **not** provide root access, so keep
  everything inside /home/user.)
truth:
<!-- ground-truth expected by the automated test suite -->
  <expected_files>
    <file path="/home/user/output/active_users.csv"><![CDATA[
username,email,uid
alice,alice@example.com,1001
bob,bob@example,1002
dave,dave@example.com,1004
frank,frank@example.com,1006
heidi,heidi@sample.com,1008
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/output/pipeline_errors.log"><![CDATA[
INACTIVE: carol,carol@example.com,1003,false
INACTIVE: grace,grace@example.com,1007,false
ERROR: eve,eve@example.com,,true
ERROR: invalidlinewithoutcommas
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/output/summary.json"><![CDATA[
{"total_lines": 9, "valid_active": 5, "inactive": 2, "invalid": 2}
]]></file>
  </expected_files>
